settings.title: A New Low-Intervention Flight Joins the List
settings.excerpt: Three skin-contact and natural pours, chosen to show what minimal winemaking actually tastes like.
settings.body:
We have wanted to do a proper natural-wine flight for a while, and it is finally on at Corkwell. Three two-ounce pours, served side by side, so you can compare without committing to a full glass.

The flight opens with a cloudy Slovenian ribolla gialla, moves to a juicy Beaujolais gamay, and finishes on a skin-contact pinot gris from Oregon. Each one is made with native yeast and little to no added sulphur.

What low intervention actually means

The phrase gets used loosely, so here is how we mean it. These wines are made with as little added or removed as possible. The grapes ferment with the yeast that arrives on their own skins rather than a packaged strain, and little or no sulphur is added to stabilise them. The ribolla is left on its skins for a stretch, which is where its cloudiness and gentle grip come from. None of this makes a wine automatically better, but it does make it taste alive in a way worth trying.

We chose these three because they show a range rather than one idea. Set next to each other, the differences are obvious even if you have never tasted natural wine before.

How to taste the flight

  • Start with the ribolla while your palate is fresh and pick up its texture.
  • Move to the gamay for brightness and red fruit in the middle.
  • Finish on the pinot gris, where the skin contact shows most clearly.

It is priced at fourteen and changes roughly every six weeks as bottles run out. Ask the bar team which is pouring tonight, and tell us if it is your first natural-wine flight so we can talk you through what you are tasting.
